set.description: Reads or writes the value of a variable. If value is specified, sets the variable to that value and returns it; otherwise returns the variable's current value. This is the primary way to create or modify variables in Tcl.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to read or set.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: Optional. The value to assign to the variable. If omitted, the current value is returned.

set.example:
set voltage 1.2
set current $voltage
puts $current  ;# prints: 1.2

incr.description: Increments the value of a variable by the given amount. The variable must contain an integer string. If increment is omitted, it defaults to 1. Use a negative increment to decrement.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable holding an integer value.

parameters.name: increment
parameters.desc: Optional. The amount to add (default 1). Can be negative.

incr.example:
set count 0
incr count       ;# count is now 1
incr count 5     ;# count is now 6
incr count -2    ;# count is now 4

append.description: Appends all of the value arguments to the current value of the variable named by varName. If the variable does not exist, it is created. This is efficient for building large strings incrementally.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to append to. Created if it does not exist.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: One or more string values to append to the variable.

append.example:
set result "Mesh: "
append result "region1" " " "region2"
puts $result  ;# prints: Mesh: region1 region2

lappend.description: Appends each value argument as a separate list element to the variable named by varName. If the variable does not exist, it is created as a list. Treats the variable's current value as a list.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Name of the variable to append list elements to. Created if it does not exist.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: One or more values to append as individual list elements.

variable.description: Creates and optionally sets one or more namespace variables. Typically used inside a namespace eval block to define variables that belong to that namespace. Each name-value pair sets the variable; a name without a value declares it without initialization.

parameters.name: name
parameters.desc: Variable name to create or set within the current namespace.

parameters.name: value
parameters.desc: Optional. Value to assign to the variable.

unset.description: Deletes one or more variables. By default, an error is raised if a variable does not exist. Use -nocomplain to suppress errors for missing variables.

parameters.name: -nocomplain
parameters.desc: Optional. If specified, no error is raised when a variable does not exist.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: One or more variable names to delete.

unset.example:
set temp 42
unset temp
unset -nocomplain nonexistent_var

proc.description: Defines a new Tcl procedure (function). The procedure can be called by name with the specified arguments. The args parameter defines the parameter list, and body is the script to execute when the procedure is called.

parameters.name: name
parameters.desc: Name of the procedure to define.

parameters.name: args
parameters.desc: Parameter list. Can include default values (e.g., {x {y 0}}). Use 'args' as the last parameter to capture variable-length arguments.

parameters.name: body
parameters.desc: The Tcl script to execute when the procedure is called.

rename.description: Renames a command from oldName to newName. If newName is an empty string, the command is deleted. Useful for wrapping existing commands with custom logic.

parameters.name: oldName
parameters.desc: The current name of the command to rename.

parameters.name: newName
parameters.desc: The new name for the command. Use an empty string to delete the command.

global.description: Makes one or more global variables accessible within a procedure scope. Without this command, procedures have their own local scope and cannot directly access global variables.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: One or more global variable names to access inside a procedure.

upvar.description: Creates a local variable (myVar) that is linked to a variable in a different scope (otherVar). This allows procedures to modify variables in the caller's scope, similar to pass-by-reference in other languages.

parameters.name: level
parameters.desc: Optional. The scope level (default 1, meaning the caller's scope). Use #0 for global scope.

parameters.name: otherVar
parameters.desc: The variable name in the target scope to link to.

parameters.name: myVar
parameters.desc: The local variable name that will be linked to otherVar.

upvar.example:
proc double_value {varName} {
  upvar 1 $varName var
  set var [expr {$var * 2}]
}
set x 5
double_value x
puts $x  ;# prints: 10

uplevel.description: Executes a script in a different scope level. All arguments are concatenated and evaluated in the context of the given level. Useful for writing control structures that need to run code in the caller's scope.

parameters.name: level
parameters.desc: Optional. The scope level (default 1). Use #0 for global scope.

parameters.name: script
parameters.desc: One or more script fragments to concatenate and evaluate in the target scope.

catch.description: Catches exceptions from the script. Returns 0 if the script completes normally, or 1 (or other error codes) if an error occurred. Optionally stores the result or error message and error options in the named variables.

parameters.name: script
parameters.desc: The Tcl script to execute and monitor for errors.

parameters.name: resultVarName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the script's return value or error message.

parameters.name: optionsVarName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the error options dictionary (error code, stack trace, etc.).

error.description: Generates an error exception with the given message. Optionally provides additional stack trace info and an error code. Commonly used in procedures to signal invalid input or unexpected conditions.

parameters.name: message
parameters.desc: The error message string to display.

parameters.name: info
parameters.desc: Optional. Additional information appended to the error stack trace.

parameters.name: code
parameters.desc: Optional. Machine-readable error code (e.g., ARITH DIVZERO).

try.description: Provides structured exception handling (Tcl 8.6+). The try block is executed first. If an error occurs, the catch handlers are tested in order. The finally block, if present, is always executed regardless of errors.

parameters.name: script
parameters.desc: The script to execute in the try block.

parameters.name: catch pattern body
parameters.desc: Optional. One or more catch clauses. pattern matches against the error dictionary; body runs on match.

parameters.name: finally body
parameters.desc: Optional. Script that always executes, whether an error occurred or not. Useful for cleanup.

throw.description: Throws a typed exception with the given type and message (Tcl 8.6+). This is a convenience command for generating structured errors that can be caught by type-specific catch handlers.

parameters.name: type
parameters.desc: The error type identifier (e.g., ARITH, POSIX, or a custom type).

parameters.name: message
parameters.desc: Human-readable error message.

expr.description: Evaluates a mathematical or logical expression. All arguments are concatenated into a single expression string and evaluated. Supports arithmetic (+, -, *, /, %), comparison (==, !=, <, >), logical (&&, ||, !), bitwise (&, |, ^), and math functions (sin, cos, sqrt, pow, etc.). Always brace expressions for correctness and performance.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: One or more expression fragments that are concatenated and evaluated as a single expression.

expr.example:
set x 3.0
set y [expr {$x * $x + 2*$x + 1}]  ;# y = 16.0
set angle [expr {3.14159 / 4}]
set sine [expr {sin($angle)}]

subst.description: Performs command, variable, and backslash substitutions on the given string. Flags can selectively disable specific substitution types. Unlike double-quoting, subst performs substitutions at the time it is called.

parameters.name: -nobackslashes
parameters.desc: Optional. Disable backslash substitutions.

parameters.name: -nocommands
parameters.desc: Optional. Disable command substitutions ([...]).

parameters.name: -novariables
parameters.desc: Optional. Disable variable substitutions ($var).

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The string on which to perform substitutions.

subst.example:
set name "World"
set result [subst {Hello $name, 2+2=[expr {2+2}]}]
puts $result  ;# prints: Hello World, 2+2=4

string.description: Performs string operations. Common options include: length, index, range, replace, tolower, toupper, trim, match, compare, first, last, is, repeat, reverse, and map. This is one of the most frequently used Tcl commands for text processing.

parameters.name: option
parameters.desc: The string operation to perform (e.g., length, index, range, replace, match, compare, tolower, toupper, trim).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Arguments specific to the chosen option.

string.example:
string length "Silicon"      ;# returns 7
string toupper "nmos"          ;# returns NMOS
string range "0.00123" 2 end    ;# returns 00123
string match "*.cmd" "test_des.cmd"  ;# returns 1

format.description: Formats strings using C-style sprintf formatting. The formatString contains format specifiers (%d, %f, %s, %e, %x, etc.) that are replaced by the corresponding argument values.

parameters.name: formatString
parameters.desc: A format string with C-style specifiers: %d (integer), %f (float), %e (scientific), %s (string), %x (hex), etc.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Values to substitute into the format specifiers.

format.example:
set v 1.234
set i 42
puts [format "Voltage = %.3f V, Step = %d" $v $i]
;# prints: Voltage = 1.234 V, Step = 42

scan.description: Parses a string according to a format string (like C sscanf). Extracts values from the input string and optionally stores them in the named variables. Returns the number of successfully matched items.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The input string to parse.

parameters.name: formatString
parameters.desc: C-style format specifiers (%d, %f, %s, etc.) describing the expected input format.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable names to store the parsed values.

scan.example:
scan "Vg=1.2 Vd=0.5" "Vg=%f Vd=%f" vg vd
puts "Gate: $vg, Drain: $vd"  ;# prints: Gate: 1.2, Drain: 0.5

regexp.description: Matches a regular expression against a string. Returns 1 if a match is found, 0 otherwise. If match variables are specified, they are set to the matched text and any captured subexpressions. Supports standard regex syntax.

parameters.name: -nocase
parameters.desc: Optional. Case-insensitive matching.

parameters.name: -all
parameters.desc: Optional. Match as many times as possible.

parameters.name: -inline
parameters.desc: Optional. Return the match data as a list instead of setting variables.

parameters.name: -line
parameters.desc: Optional. Make ^ and $ match beginning/end of each line, not the whole string.

parameters.name: exp
parameters.desc: The regular expression pattern.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The string to search.

parameters.name: matchVar
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable to store the full match.

parameters.name: subMatchVar
parameters.desc: Optional. Variables to store captured subexpression matches.

regsub.description: Performs regular expression substitution on a string. Replaces the first (or all with -all) match of the pattern with the substitution specification. Returns the number of matches found.

parameters.name: -all
parameters.desc: Optional. Replace all occurrences, not just the first.

parameters.name: -nocase
parameters.desc: Optional. Case-insensitive matching.

parameters.name: exp
parameters.desc: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The input string to process.

parameters.name: subSpec
parameters.desc: The replacement string. Use & for the matched text, \1-\9 for submatches.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable to store the result. If omitted, the result is returned.

regsub.example:
set path "/home/user/sim/run1_des.cmd"
regsub -all {/} $path "\\" winpath
puts $winpath  ;# prints: \home\user\sim\run1_des.cmd

list.description: Creates a list from its arguments. Each argument becomes a separate element of the list. This is the canonical way to create well-formed lists, as it handles elements containing spaces or special characters correctly.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Zero or more values to become list elements. If no arguments, returns an empty list.

list.example:
set materials [list Silicon Oxide "Aluminum (99.99%)"]
puts $materials  ;# prints: Silicon Oxide {Aluminum (99.99%)}

llength.description: Returns the number of elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list whose element count to return.

llength.example:
set layers [list SiO2 Si Al]
puts [llength $layers]  ;# prints: 3

lindex.description: Returns an element from a list by its index. Indices are zero-based. Supports multi-level indexing for nested lists. Use end for the last element, end-N for offsets from the end.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list to index into.

parameters.name: index
parameters.desc: Zero-based index. Use 'end' for the last element, 'end-N' for offset from end.

linsert.description: Returns a new list with the specified elements inserted at the given index position. The original list is not modified. Elements before index remain in place; new elements appear at position index.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The original list.

parameters.name: index
parameters.desc: The position at which to insert. Use 0 for the beginning, end for appending.

parameters.name: element
parameters.desc: One or more elements to insert at the specified index.

linsert.example:
set mats [list Si Al]
set result [linsert $mats 1 Oxide]
puts $result  ;# prints: Si Oxide Al

lrange.description: Returns a sublist consisting of elements from first to last index (inclusive). Use 0 for the first element and end for the last.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The source list.

parameters.name: first
parameters.desc: Index of the first element to include (0-based). Use 'end' for the last.

parameters.name: last
parameters.desc: Index of the last element to include. Use 'end' for the last.

lreplace.description: Returns a new list with elements from first to last replaced by the given elements. If no replacement elements are specified, the range is deleted. The original list is not modified.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The original list.

parameters.name: first
parameters.desc: Index of the first element to replace.

parameters.name: last
parameters.desc: Index of the last element to replace.

parameters.name: element
parameters.desc: Optional. Replacement elements. If omitted, the specified range is deleted.

lsearch.description: Searches a list for an element matching the pattern. Returns the index of the first match (-1 if not found). The matching mode can be -exact (string equality), -glob (wildcard patterns, default), or -regexp (regular expressions).

parameters.name: -exact
parameters.desc: Optional. Match by exact string comparison.

parameters.name: -glob
parameters.desc: Optional. Match using glob patterns (default).

parameters.name: -regexp
parameters.desc: Optional. Match using regular expressions.

parameters.name: -all
parameters.desc: Optional. Return a list of all matching indices.

parameters.name: -inline
parameters.desc: Optional. Return the matching values instead of indices.

parameters.name: -not
parameters.desc: Optional. Invert the match (return non-matching elements).

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list to search.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.desc: The pattern to match against list elements.

lsort.description: Sorts a list and returns a new sorted list. Supports multiple sorting modes: ASCII (default), dictionary (case-insensitive), integer, and real numeric. The original list is not modified.

parameters.name: -ascii
parameters.desc: Optional. Sort using ASCII order (default).

parameters.name: -dictionary
parameters.desc: Optional. Case-insensitive dictionary sort.

parameters.name: -integer
parameters.desc: Optional. Sort by integer value.

parameters.name: -real
parameters.desc: Optional. Sort by floating-point value.

parameters.name: -increasing
parameters.desc: Optional. Sort in ascending order (default).

parameters.name: -decreasing
parameters.desc: Optional. Sort in descending order.

parameters.name: -command cmd
parameters.desc: Optional. Use a custom comparison command for sorting.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list to sort.

split.description: Splits a string into a list of substrings using the specified split characters. Each character in splitChars is treated as a separate delimiter. If splitChars is omitted, whitespace is used as the default delimiter.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The string to split.

parameters.name: splitChars
parameters.desc: Optional. Characters used as delimiters (each character is a separate delimiter). Default is whitespace.

split.example:
set line "x=1.0 y=2.0 z=3.0"
set parts [split $line " "]
puts [llength $parts]  ;# prints: 3
puts [split "a,b,c" ","]  ;# prints: a b c

join.description: Joins the elements of a list into a single string, with each element separated by the join string. If joinString is omitted, a single space is used. This is the inverse of the split command.

parameters.name: list
parameters.desc: The list whose elements to join.

parameters.name: joinString
parameters.desc: Optional. The separator string placed between elements. Default is a single space.

open.description: Opens a file or command pipeline and returns a channel identifier. The access mode controls reading/writing: r (read), w (write), a (append), r+ (read-write), etc. Use '|' prefix to open a pipeline.

parameters.name: fileName
parameters.desc: The file path to open. Prefix with '|' to open a command pipeline.

parameters.name: access
parameters.desc: Optional. Access mode: r (read, default), w (write), a (append), r+ (read-write), w+ (read-write truncate).

parameters.name: permissions
parameters.desc: Optional. POSIX permission bits (e.g., 0644) for newly created files.

open.example:
set fid [open "output.dat" w]
puts $fid "Simulation results"
close $fid

set fid [open "input.dat" r]
set data [read $fid]
close $fid

close.description: Closes an open channel (file, pipe, or socket). Always close channels when done to free resources and flush buffers. For pipeline channels, waits for the subprocess to complete.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: The channel identifier returned by open or socket.

close.example:
set fid [open "data.txt" r]
set content [read $fid]
close $fid

read.description: Reads data from a channel. If numChars is specified, reads exactly that many characters; otherwise reads all remaining data until end-of-file. Use -nonewline flag to strip the trailing newline.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: The channel to read from.

parameters.name: numChars
parameters.desc: Optional. Number of characters to read. If omitted, reads all remaining data.

read.example:
set fid [open "params.txt" r]
set content [read $fid]
close $fid

;# Read first 100 characters
set fid [open "log.txt" r]
set header [read $fid 100]
close $fid

puts.description: Writes a string to a channel, followed by a newline (unless -nonewline is specified). If channelId is omitted, writes to stdout. This is the primary output command in Tcl.

parameters.name: -nonewline
parameters.desc: Optional. Suppress the trailing newline.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: Optional. The channel to write to (default: stdout). Use a file channel ID for file output.

parameters.name: string
parameters.desc: The string to write.

puts.example:
puts "Simulation complete"          ;# to stdout
puts -nonewline "Enter value: "
set fid [open "log.txt" a]
puts $fid "Step 1: converged"
close $fid

gets.description: Reads a single line from a channel. If varName is specified, stores the line in that variable and returns the number of characters read (-1 at end-of-file). If varName is omitted, returns the line itself.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: The channel to read from.

parameters.name: varName
parameters.desc: Optional. Variable name to store the line. Returns character count instead of the line itself.

flush.description: Flushes the output buffer of a channel, forcing any buffered data to be written. Useful when writing to files or pipes and you need to ensure data is persisted immediately.

parameters.name: channelId
parameters.desc: The channel whose output buffer to flush.

flush.example:
set fid [open "progress.log" w]
puts $fid "Starting simulation..."
flush $fid  ;# ensure it's written immediately

file.description: Performs file and directory operations. Common options: exists (check existence), readable/writable (check permissions), size (file size), dirname/basename/tail/rootname/extension (path parts), mkdir/delete/rename/copy (file operations), isdirectory/isfile (type checks), join (path construction).

parameters.name: option
parameters.desc: The file operation: exists, readable, writable, size, dirname, basename, tail, extension, mkdir, delete, rename, copy, isdirectory, isfile, join, nativename, etc.

parameters.name: name
parameters.desc: File or directory path.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Additional arguments specific to the chosen option.

glob.description: Returns a list of file names matching the given glob patterns. Supports wildcards: * (any string), ? (single char), [abc] (character set). Use -directory to search in a specific directory, -types to filter by file type (f for files, d for directories).

parameters.name: -nocomplain
parameters.desc: Optional. Return empty list instead of error when no files match.

parameters.name: -directory dir
parameters.desc: Optional. Search in the specified directory.

parameters.name: -types typeList
parameters.desc: Optional. Filter by type: f (file), d (directory), l (symlink).

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.desc: One or more glob patterns (e.g., *.cmd, *_des.cmd).

source.description: Reads and evaluates a Tcl script from a file. The file's contents are executed as a Tcl script in the current scope. Commonly used to load configuration files, libraries, or shared procedures.

parameters.name: fileName
parameters.desc: Path to the Tcl script file to evaluate.

info.description: Returns information about the Tcl interpreter state. Common options: exists (variable existence), level (procedure call stack), procs (list of procedures), body/args (procedure details), commands (list of commands), vars (variable names), hostname, patchlevel (Tcl version).

parameters.name: option
parameters.desc: The information option: exists, level, procs, body, args, commands, vars, hostname, patchlevel, script, etc.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Arguments specific to the chosen option.

array.description: Performs operations on Tcl arrays (key-value maps). Common options: exists (check if array exists), get (return key-value pairs), names (return key names), set (initialize from a list), size (number of elements), unset (delete array), startsearch/nextelement/anymore/donesearch (iterators).

parameters.name: option
parameters.desc: The array operation: exists, get, names, set, size, unset, startsearch, nextelement, anymore, donesearch.

parameters.name: arrayName
parameters.desc: Name of the array to operate on.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.desc: Arguments specific to the chosen option.
